coredata.dc:description: The pesticide residues of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) in the environment poses a threat to human health and the ecological environment. Consequently, creating a dependable and effective platform for the detection of OPs is crucial for safeguarding food safety, preserving public health, and upholding ecological equilibrium. Herein, we synthesized bimetallic nanoflowers (AP-PdmPtn NFs) with core-shell structure for the first time and their particle size was about 18 nm. AP-PdmPtn NFs facilitated the conversion of the colorless chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB), demonstrating their remarkable oxidase-like activity. The kinetics of the catalytic process adhered to the conventional Michaelis-Menten equation. In addition, we synthesized 1.36 nm red carbon dots (CDs) that can be quenched by oxTMB. Importantly, we developed a dual-mode OPs biosensor based on AP-PdPt NFs and red CDs. The biosensor can produce colorimetric and fluorescent signals for OPs in fruits and vegetables solution, and the limits of detection for dimethoate were 0.37 ng/mL in colorimetric method and 0.41 ng/mL using fluorescent mode, respectively. This dual-mode biosensor was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of dimethoate in real samples, which develops a new strategy for OPs detection.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode biosensor based on Aloe vera polysaccharide stabilized core-shell nanoflowers and red carbon dots for organophosphorus pesticide detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pesticide residues of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) in the environment poses a threat to human health and the ecological environment. Consequently, creating a dependable and effective platform for the detection of OPs is crucial for safeguarding food safety, preserving public health, and upholding ecological equilibrium. Herein, we synthesized bimetallic nanoflowers (AP-PdmPtn NFs) with core-shell structure for the first time and their particle size was about 18 nm. AP-PdmPtn NFs facilitated the conversion of the colorless chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB), demonstrating their remarkable oxidase-like activity. The kinetics of the catalytic process adhered to the conventional Michaelis-Menten equation. In addition, we synthesized 1.36 nm red carbon dots (CDs) that can be quenched by oxTMB. Importantly, we developed a dual-mode OPs biosensor based on AP-PdPt NFs and red CDs. The biosensor can produce colorimetric and fluorescent signals for OPs in fruits and vegetables solution, and the limits of detection for dimethoate were 0.37 ng/mL in colorimetric method and 0.41 ng/mL using fluorescent mode, respectively. This dual-mode biosensor was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of dimethoate in real samples, which develops a new strategy for OPs detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) play an indispensable role in agriculture, forestry, and animal husbandry [1,2]. However, the utilization rate of pesticides is only 15 %, resulting in the production of pesticide residues in crops, which may not only affect human health through the food chain, but also accumulate in the environment, especially in soil and water bodies, creating a significant risk to the ecological environment. Research indicates that one of the ways in which OPs exert their effects is through their interaction with acetylcholinesterase (AChE), leading to the formation of phosphorylated cholinesterase [3,4]. Such irreversible damage to the nervous system can result in a variety of toxic symptoms, including difficulty in breathing, uncontrollable movements, loss of consciousness, and potentially fatal outcomes [1,5,6]. Therefore, the establishment of an efficient and reliable OPs testing platform plays a vital role in ensuring food safety, protecting public health and maintaining ecological balance.

To date, a variety of sophisticated detection techniques have been developed in the field of pesticide residue analysis, including gas chromatography [7], liquid chromatography [8] and chromatography-mass spectrometry [9,10]. They provide sensitive and accurate detection of pesticide residues in agricultural products and environmental samples. However, the instruments in the above detection methods are expensive. The samples need complex pretreatment, separation, and then tested and quantified by the instrument. The above process not only prolongs the detection cycle, but also greatly increases the time cost and transportation cost, and the application conditions are harsh. These shortcomings limit the wide range of applications for this type of detection method [11]. Consequently, researchers have shown significant interest in biosensors that offer high specificity, affordability, and ease of use. To date, electrochemical and various optical biosensors have been applied to pesticide detection [12–15]. However, these instruments are expensive and unstable, which is not conducive to the detection of variable environments in the field [16]. For example, the complexity of the preparation of enzyme sensing electrodes for electrochemical sensors makes it difficult to ensure the performance and reproducibility of the sensors. Disadvantages such as poor stability and persistence of enzyme activity, which limit the development of electrochemical enzyme sensors. With advances in nanotechnology, nanomaterials have revolutionized the field of biosensors, especially in improving sensor performance and opening up new applications [17–19].

As a category of nanomaterials that emulate the catalytic functions of natural enzymes, nanozymes are often referred to as “artificial enzymes” [20]. These substances are characterized by their ease of synthesis, high stability, and cost-effectiveness. Additionally, their catalytic properties can be tailored by manipulating their morphology and dimensions [21]. Consequently, nanozymes find extensive applications in fields such as environmental remediation, medical diagnostics, and biological therapies [22–25]. In recent years, numerous biosensors utilizing nanozymes have been developed and employed for the analysis of OPs [26]. These nanozymes can be classified into four primary categories based on their metal content [27–30], metal oxide-based nanozymes [31,32], carbon-based nanozymes [33–35], and metal organic framework (MOFs) nanozymes [36,37]. Among these, nanozymes based on noble metals have garnered significant interest owing to their stable structures and straightforward synthesis processes [28]. In a previous study, we prepared Pt nanoflowers with good oxidase-like activity for the sensitive detection of glyphosate pesticides [38]. It was found that alloying with Ag or Au resulted in a decrease in peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activity [39], while proper alloying with Pd could effectively enhance the oxidase-like activity of Pt NPs in a component-dependent manner [40–42]. In contrast to monometallic catalysts, the d electrons in PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles undergo a transfer from the Pt 5d orbitals to the Pd 4d orbitals following the alloying process and/or the hybridization of the two metals. This electronic state broadening plays a crucial role in enhancing the catalytic efficiency of the bimetallic PtPd catalyst [43,44]. Furthermore, the structural distribution of the metal components has also contributed significantly to the improved catalytic performance observed in this catalyst [45]. The implementation of a core-shell architecture notably boosts the chemical stability of the catalyst. Furthermore, findings indicate that the thickness of the platinum shell can be accurately regulated through meticulous adjustments to the reaction parameters [46], and the change of electronic structure caused by the change of nanoparticles composition provided a large space for regulating the activity of enzymes [47]. Therefore, it is promising to explore two-component metal nanostructures to optimize enzyme-like catalytic efficiency.

Carbon dots are essentially tiny particles of semiconductors whose size is measured in nanometers. Their remarkable electrical and optical characteristics have drawn the attention of researchers, leading to their application in sensing and biosensing technologies. These materials are particularly well-suited for the fabrication of nanodevices and biosensors designed to detect pesticides across a range of samples. In certain instances, the effectiveness of sensing can be enhanced by integrating nanomaterials with carbon dots. One such study utilized a combination of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and zirconium ferrocene metal-organic framework nanosheets (FMOF-Zr) to create a multifunctional sensing platform mediated by nanozymes. This platform exhibited multiple signal responses, including colorimetry, fluorescence, and temperature changes, enabling the visual, sensitive, and portable detection of glyphosate (GLP). Notably, the peroxidase-like activity of FMOF-Zr experienced considerable inhibition as a result of the blocking effect caused by various interactions between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, leading to increased sensitivity of the biosensor. This approach demonstrates both high sensitivity and low detection limits. [48].

In this work, we used Aloe vera polysaccharide (AP) as stabilizer and reducing agent to prepare PdPt core-shell nanoflowers (AP-PdmPtn NFs) with different metal ratios. AP-PdmPtn NFs exhibit excellent oxidase-like activity and are smaller in size than monometallic nanoflowers (AP-Ptn NFs). In addition, we prepared carbon dots (CDs) that emit red light and studied the properties and fluorescence mechanism of CDs. Based on the oxididase-like properties of AP-PdmPtn NFs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs, a colorimetric fluorescent biosensor for organophosphorus pesticides was established. The application of AP-PdmPtn NFs in the detection of organophosphorus pesticides in actual samples was further discussed. As a noble metal nanozyme with oxidase-like activity, AP-PdmPtn NFs have a good application prospect in the field of organophosphorus pesticide detection and analysis.
The specific information for materials and apparatus can be found in the supporting information.

1 mL of AP solution (2 mg/mL) was mixed with Na2PdCl4 (2 mM) in a PE tube, incubated at 60 °C for 30 min, and 25 μL of ascorbic acid (0.05 M) was added to the thermomixer for 3 h at 60 °C. The mixed solution was dialyzed for 24 h to remove excess reducing agent, Na+ and Cl−. The dialysis solution was transferred to a PE tube, K2PtCl4 (2 mM) was added and incubated in a thermomixer for 30 min, and 25 μL of ascorbic acid (0.05 M) was added for 6 h to obtain AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs. Finally, AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs were transferred to dialysis bags with molecular weight 8000–14,000 Da for 24 h to remove excess reducing agent, Na+ and Cl−. The molar ratio of AP to metal elements (Pd, Pt) is 1:19, and Pd:Pt is m:n (m = 1, n = 1, 5, 10). The preparation methods of AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were consistent with those of AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs.
The preparation process of AP-Pt NFs and AP-Pd NPs was similar to that described in previously work [38].

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared according to the literature with some modifications [49]. 80 mg of potassium periodate (KIO4) and 20 mg of p-phenylenediamine were weighed, the weighed drug was transferred to a beaker, 20 mL of absolute ethanol was added and stirred until p-phenylenediamine was completely dissolved, and the solution was transferred to a 90 mL autoclave for 2 h at 180 °C. A dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 10,000 Da was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain red carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of oxidase-like activity of AP-PdmPtn NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-like activity of AP-PdmPtn NFs was evaluated by quantifying the amount of oxidized TMB, and the experimental method was similar to previous work [38].

Additionally, absorbance values were recorded at pH levels ranging from 1 to 12 and temperatures from 20 to 70 °C. The concentrations of AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs were CPd = 0.105 mM and CPt = 0.116 mM, the concentrations of AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs were CPd = 0.075 mM and CPt = 0.397 mM, and the concentrations of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were CPd = 0.058 mM and CPt = 0.608 mM. The above concentrations of metal elements are the test results of inductively coupled plasma (ICP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs and CDs for organophosphorus pesticide detection
ce:section.ce:para: The use of AP-PdmPtn NFs for colorimetric detection of dimethoate was evaluated by assessing the absorbance of oxTMB. Initially, 50 μL of glyphosate solution was mixed with 50 μL of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) at a concentration of 150 U/L in a polyethylene tube, followed by a 15-min incubation period. After this, 150 μL of acetylcholine (ACh) at a concentration of 1 mM was added, and the mixture was incubated for an additional 30 min, resulting in the formation of the OPs-AChE-ACh solution. This solution was then combined with 200 μL of AP-PdmPtn NFs (with a concentration of CPt = 0.61 mM) and 50 μL of a buffer solution containing acetic acid and sodium acetate (0.2 M, pH 4). Subsequently, 1 mL of TMB (0.6 mM, pH 4) was added to the mixture. The reaction was carried out in a thermomixer at 40 °C for 10 min. Finally, the absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at a wavelength of 652 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The use of AP-PdmPtn NFs for the fluorescence detection analysis of dimethoate was determined by measuring the emission wavelength of CDs at 608 nm. On the basis of the above colorimetric detection operation, 1 mL of the prepared CDs solution was added and mixed. The emission wavelength of the mixture at 608 nm was determined with a fluorescence spectrophotometer after 1 min.

When setting up the experiment of the interference control group, some non-organophosphorus pesticides were selected as interfering agents, including fenvalerate, tebuconazole, thiamethoxam, glucose and fructose, as well as some interfering ions (K+, Zn2+, Mg2+), which were determined instead of glyphosate solution according to the above experimental methods. The concentration of each interfering substance was 100 mg/L.

Through the recovery test, lettuce, cucumber and melon were taken as the research objects, and the accuracy of the method for the detection of OPs was studied. Among them, lettuce, cucumber and melon are purchased in local supermarkets, and the lettuce, cucumber and melon are crushed first, the supernatant is centrifuged and diluted 10 times with deionized water. Then, different amounts of glyphosate were added. Finally, these spiked samples were tested with the glyphosate colorimetric method described above. Three parallel samples were measured each time.

Five samples of AP-Pd NPs, AP-Pt NFs, AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs, AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were synthesized using AP as reducing agent and stabilizer, respectively, and the AP-Pd NPs and AP-Pt NFs were used as the control group, and the nanoflowers were characterized. As shown in Fig. 1a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Na2PdCl4 has a corresponding characteristic absorption peak at 425 nm and K2PtCl4 has a low-intensity narrow-band absorption at 300 to 450 nm, which is attributed to Pd2+ and Pt2+, respectively. When the metal precursor solution was co-incubated with ascorbic acid and AP, the above characteristic peaks disappeared, indicating that Pd2+ and Pt2+ disappeared and were reduced to zero-valent Pd and Pt by the reducing agent. Among them, AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs had the highest absorbance. It can also be seen in Fig. 1b that the physical colors of AP-Pt NPs and AP-Pd NFs are black and brownish-yellow, and the color gradually deepens to black with the increase of different PdPt molar ratios.

The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of AP-Pt NPs, AP-Pd NFs, AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs, AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were obtained by a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90. As shown in Fig. 1c, the hydrodynamic size of bimetallic nanozymes is smaller than that of monometallic nanozymes, and the hydrodynamic size decreases with the increase of molar ratio Pd to Pt, and the zeta potential of bimetallic nanozymes is greater than that of monometallic nanozymes, indicating that bimetallic nanozymes are more stable than monometallic nanozymes. The hydrodynamic sizes of AP-Pt NPs, AP-Pd NFs, AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs, AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs, and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs are 60.18 ± 2.34 nm, 69.47 ± 2.33 nm, 42.23 ± 7.19 nm, 41.53 ± 3.81 nm, and 34.57 ± 7.05 nm, respectively. The reduction of hydrodynamic size may be attributed to the effect of bimetallic molar ratio regulation on the particle size of nanoparticles. Aloe vera polysaccharides are electronegative due to their large number of electronegative groups, which is consistent with what has been reported in the literature [50]. In Fig. 1d, the zeta potentials for AP-Pt NPs, AP-Pd NFs, AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs, AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs, and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs are −11.90 ± 1.80 mV, −13.33 ± 2.15 mV, −16.97 ± 2.60 mV, −16.87 ± 1.06 mV, and −17.67 ± 0.25 mV, respectively. In addition, AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs also had good stability, which were stable for 7 days in the liquid state (Fig. S9). The excellent stability can be attributed to the spatial steric hindrance effect of AP. As a natural polymer with abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, AP not only has a high absolute negative zeta potential, which results in strong electrostatic repulsion, but also forms a physical protective layer around the nanomaterials through its complex 3D network structure. This result echoes and complements the zeta potential in Fig. 1d. This three-dimensional hindering effect effectively prevents the tight aggregation of nanoscale flower-like structures even under long-term liquid storage conditions. The synergistic effect of electrostatic repulsion and spatial confinement ensures that the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs remain well dispersed, which is consistent with the stability performance and further highlights the dual stabilizing role of AP in nanomaterial systems.

The morphology of the synthesized AP-PdmPtn NFs was examined using transmission electron microscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1e–g, the particle sizes for AP-Pd1Pt1 NFs, AP-Pd1Pt5 NFs, and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs are measured at 20.79 ± 5.22 nm, 18.42 ± 2.19 nm, and 17.86 ± 2.44 nm, respectively. Compared with the monometallic AP-Ptn NFs in the previous paper [38], the particle size of bimetallic nanoflowers decreased, indicating that the introduction of Pd was helpful to improve the morphology and specific surface area of nanoflowers, but the improvement of its properties needs to be further studied. As shown in Fig. S1, the particle sizes of AP-Pt NFs and AP-Pd NPs in the control group were 43.98 ± 4.67 nm and 10.56 ± 2.08 nm, respectively.

Through Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis, we can effectively characterize the molecular structure and chemical composition of AP and AP-PdmPtn NFs. Fig. 1h shows the infrared absorption spectra of AP and AP-PdmPtn NFs, where four main absorption peaks are observed at 3276 cm−1, 2935 cm−1, 1652 cm−1, and 1016 cm−1, respectively. Notably, the characteristic absorption peaks of AP are notably found at 3276 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of the OH and CH bonds, respectively. Additionally, the peak observed at 1652 cm−1 is linked to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl group (C=O) within the molecule, potentially arising from either an ester or a ketone functional group present in AP. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 1016 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the COC glycosidic bond, providing further evidence for the existence of a glycosidic bond structure in the AP molecule. The infrared spectra of AP and AP-PdmPtn NFs show consistency in the positions of the absorption peaks, suggesting that the Pt NFs are attached to AP and have a stable structure. The stability of this structure is crucial for the potential applications of AP-PdmPtn NFs, as it may affect their biocompatibility, bioactivity, and functional performance in areas such as biosensing.

Additionally, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) results further confirm the synthesis of PdPt NPs. As shown in Fig. 1i, AP-PdmPtn NFs exhibit five diffraction signals at 2θ, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes, which align with the standard diffraction patterns of Pt and Pd NPs. Furthermore, as the molar ratio of Pt to Pd metal increases, the diffraction peaks shift slightly to the left, indicating that we have successfully synthesized PdPt nanoparticles.

AP-PdmPtn NFs are used as biological templates to synthesize organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterials with good dispersion by hydrothermally using AP macromolecules as biological templates. As shown in Fig. S2, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) characterization showed that PdmPtn NFs were synthesized on the surface of AP, and the nanoflowers were slightly aggregated on the surface of the polysaccharides during the freeze-drying operation.

In addition, the Pd element shown in Fig. S3 does not appear in the results, possibly because the SEM can only test elements on the surface of the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. In the process of preparation of AP-PdmPtn NFs, it is divided into two parts, firstly, Pd2+ is made to form Pd NPs on the surface of AP, and then Pt2+ is added to make PdPt nanoflowers grow on the surface of Pd nanoparticles. Therefore, the absence of Pd in the Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) test results may be due to the formation of PdPt nanoflower structures centered on Pd. Thus, we successfully fabricated AP-PdmPtn NFs composite nanomaterials through a simple self-assembly process.

To further study the structure of AP-PdmPtn NFs, we used high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) to test AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. As shown in Fig. 2a–b, AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs are composed of two metal elements, Pd and Pt, and Fig. 2c shows that Pd is located in the center of the nanoflower, and Pt atoms grow on the surface of the Pd nanoparticles. Small Pd nanoparticles are also mixed in the process of growth, but in general, Pd NPs are the nucleus, and Pt atoms grow on its surface to form the PdPt nanoflower structure. As shown in Fig. 2d, the distribution of Pd and Pt elements on the High-Angle Annular Dark-Field (HAADF) images of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs shows that most of the Pd NPs are concentrated in the interior of the nanoflowers, and Pt nanoparticles grow around the Pd NPs. Finally, the area selected in Fig. 2d is tested for element accumulation, and the results in Fig. S4 show that the atomic percentages of metal Pd and Pt are 9.79 % and 90.21 %, respectively, indicating that the molar ratio of Pd to Pt element in AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs is approximately 1:10. This is also consistent with the ICP test results.

In addition, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum analysis was employed as an effective characterization method for further analysis of the surface chemical composition of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. The results of XPS element spectrum analysis are shown in Fig. S5, revealing that AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs contain four elements: C, O, Pt, and Pd, where the presence of C and O elements can be traced back to the composition of AP, indicating that Aloe vera polysaccharides successfully attach to the surface of alloy nanoparticles. The presence of Pt and Pd elements confirms the formation of metal nanoparticles. In Fig. 2e, XPS spectrum for the Pt 4f region of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs revealed two distinct peaks at 71.3 eV and 74.5 eV, which are attributed to Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2, respectively. These findings suggest that the Pt present in the synthesized AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs exists in a zero-valent state. Furthermore, XPS analysis can also be employed to assess the uniformity of the surface composition and the elemental distribution of the nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 2f, there is no significant scattering peak in the Pd 3d orbital of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. This may be due to the fact that in the process of preparing nanoflowers, Pd nanoparticles are first reduced on the surface of AP, and the Pt2+ grows subsequently on the surface of Pd nanoparticles in the process of reduction by reducing agents. As more and more Pt nanoparticles are reduced, more and more Pt atoms grow into nanoflowers on the surface of Pd nanoparticles. This results in too few Pd nanoparticles on the surface of the nanoflowers and no obvious scattering peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and analysis of red carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: First, we used TEM to characterize the morphology and particle size of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a–b, the TEM image of CDs is monodispersed with a particle size of 1.36 ± 0.33 nm. Second, we characterize the optical properties of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs reveal the presence of two broad absorption bands at wavelengths of 384 nm and 470 nm. These bands are attributed to the n-π* transitions associated with the CC bonds and the n-π* transitions linked to either CO or CN functionalities, respectively [49]. We also studied the excitation and absorption wavelengths and the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs. The data presented in Fig. 3c indicates that CDs emit light at a wavelength of 608 nm when excited with light around 550 nm. The figure illustrates that CDs appear reddish-brown under natural lighting conditions, while they exhibit a red hue when exposed to ultraviolet light. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 3d, the maximum emission wavelength for CDs is also observed at 608 nm with an excitation wavelength of 555 nm. Therefore, it can be concluded that the most effective excitation wavelength for carbon dots is 555 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of oxidase-like activity of AP-PdmPtn NFs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the oxidase-like activity of AP-PdmPtn NFs, we established four experimental groups: [AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs + TMB], [TMB], [AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs] and [CDs + TMB]. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, the groups [TMB], [AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs], and [CDs + TMB] displayed low absorbance within the 550–800 nm range. In contrast, the [AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs + TMB] group exhibited a prominent absorption peak for oxTMB at 652 nm, confirming that AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were capable of oxidizing TMB to oxTMB.

Furthermore, Fig. 4b illustrated that all AP-PdmPtn NFs demonstrated oxidase-like activity, with AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs exhibiting the most significant activity. We also investigated how temperature and pH influence the catalytic performance of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. According to Fig. 4c, the catalytic activity peaks at a pH of 4, with a decline to below 80 % at other pH levels. Additionally, Fig. 4d showed that the optimal catalytic activity occurs at 40 °C, with a reduction in activity at other temperatures. Notably, pH appears to have a more pronounced effect on the oxidase-like activity of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs compared to temperature, likely due to the inherent characteristics of the nanofibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic kinetic analysis of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to examine the catalytic reaction kinetics of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs, we obtained kinetic data across a spectrum of TMB concentrations. As shown in Fig. S6, the kinetics recorded for TMB concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.8 mM conformed to the Michaelis-Menten model. Additionally, Fig. S7 displayed the Lineweaver-Burk equation, represented as (Y = 0.0118X + 0.0267) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.993. The determined values for the Michaelis constant (K
m ) and the maximum reaction rate (V
max ) for AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs were found to be 0.44 mM and 37.45 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. Table 1 indicated that the K
m (0.44 mM) was lower than that of other nanozymes, such as AP-Pt10 NFs (0.47 mM), Se NPs (8.30 mM), Ag3PO4MCs (3.31 mM), and ZIF-67 (13.69 mM), demonstrating a good affinity for TMB. Additionally, AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs exhibited a higher V
max (31.45 × 10−8 M s−1) compared to other nanomaterials, including AP-Pt10 NFs (13.02 × 10−8 M s−1), PdPt3-LNT NDs (2.88 × 10−8 M s−1), Pd150-PCRP NPs (15.58 × 10−8 M s−1) and Mn3O4 NFs (1.35 × 10−8 M s−1). These results indicated that AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs had superior catalytic effects and good oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalytic mechanism of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs and the quenching mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced are a key mechanism for the oxidase-like activity of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs. Similarly, we set up five experimental groups to study the types of ROS produced by AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs during catalysis. As shown in Fig. 4e, the amount of oxTMB produced in the [AP-Pd1Pt10 + TMB + NaN3] and [AP-Pd1Pt10 + TMB + BQ] groups with ROS inhibitor were reduced by 29.4 % (Cinhibitor = 1 mM) and 88.9 % (Cinhibitor = 1 mM) in the control group (without reactive oxygen species inhibitor), respectively. The catalytic oxidation reaction of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs was inhibited because NaN3 and BQ scavenge 1O2 and O2
−, respectively, indicating that oxidase activity was affected by these species. Fig. 4e showed that the oxidase-like activity of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs decreased as the concentration of ROS inhibitors increases, demonstrating that catalytic activity was influenced by the levels of 1O2 and O2
− in solution. In contrast, the oxidase-like activity in the [AP-Pd1Pt10 + TMB + IPA] and [AP-Pd1Pt10 + TMB + EDTA-2Na] groups remained unaffected, suggesting that OH and oxygen vacancies did not impact catalytic oxidation reactions. Additionally, terephthalic acid (TA) could capture OH, exhibiting fluorescent properties detectable at 425 nm. As shown in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence intensity of 2-hydroxyterephthalate at the emission wavelength at 425 nm was used to characterize the amount of OH. AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs did not produce a fluorescence signal at 425 nm after incubation with TA for 6 h, indicating that AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs did not produce OH. The oxidase-like activity observed in AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs can primarily be attributed to the generation of 1O2 and O2
−. This finding aligned with results reported in previous studies [56,57]. In summary, during the catalytic oxidation process involving AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs, the dissolved O2 in water was activated by these nanofibers along with ROS (1O2 and O2
−). The ROS then further oxidizes the TMB to oxTMB.

The mechanism by which oxTMB can quench the fluorescence of red CDs was explored by UV–Vis and fluorescence absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4g, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with oxTMB is significantly reduced, suggesting that oxTMB is able to quench the fluorescence of CDs. In a subsequent study, we explored the UV–Vis absorption peak of oxTMB and the fluorescence emission peak of CDs at the excitation wavelength of 555 nm and found that the area of the absorption peak around 652 nm of oxTMB overlapped by 72.5 % with the emission peak of CDs as shown in Fig. 4h. This is mild to the basic conditions for the internal filtration effect of fluorescent substances. Fluorescence filtering is an optical phenomenon that occurs when a fluorescent or other light-absorbing substance absorbs excitation or emitted light, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This suggests that the fluorescence quenching of CDs is due to the internal quenching effect of oxTMB and CDs. In addition, fluorescence lifetime is also an important factor affecting the fluorescence performance of CDs [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of organophosphorus pesticides based on AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs and red CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Similar to the mechanism of detection of organophosphorus pesticides in Fig. S8, AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs with oxidase-like activity oxidized colorless TMB to blue oxTMB. AChE hydrolyzes acetylcholine to generate choline, while organophosphorus pesticides inhibit AChE activity. In this context, a colorimetric fluorescent dual-mode biosensor for the organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate was developed, utilizing oxTMB to quench red CDs. In the absence of OPs, AChE hydrolyzes acetylcholine to generate reducing choline, and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs catalyzes the oxidation of O2 dissolved in water to produce ROS (1O2 and O2
−), and then ROS further oxidizes TMB to blue oxTMB, the combination of reducing choline and oxTMB leads to the reduction of oxTMB, then disappearance of blue color and the fluorescence of CDs. When pesticides inhibit AChE, reducing choline decreases, resulting in an increase in oxTMB and quenching CDs fluorescence, thus achieving colorimetric-fluorescence dual signal detection. Colorimetric detection involved measuring the absorption peak intensity of blue oxTMB, as illustrated in Fig. 5a. Increasing dimethoate concentration enhances acetylcholinesterase inhibition on acetylcholine hydrolysis, leading to reduced choline production and higher absorbance of oxTMB. Fig. 5b demonstrated a robust linear correlation between the absorbance measured at 652 nm and the logarithmic values of dimethoate concentration. The standard equation for detecting dimethoate with AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs is (A = 0.3 log C + 1.032) (R2 = 0.993). This method provides a detection range from 0.003 to 100 mg/L, along with a detection limit of 0.37 ng/mL.

In addition, on the basis of the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs colorimetric biosensor, we also introduced red-glowing CDs into the detection system of organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate and composed the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs fluorescent colorimetric biosensor based on red-light CDs. The detection principle of the biosensor for the organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate is that oxTMB at 652 nm can quench the red-glowing CDs, so that the CDs lose their fluorescence properties, and the On-Off change of CDs fluorescence is the key to the establishment of the fluorescent biosensor.

Specifically, the nanozyme AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs with oxidase-like properties can oxidize TMB to produce oxTMB, and the concentration of organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate has a good logarithmic relationship with the absorbance of oxTMB, and oxTMB can quench the red fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, we established the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs fluorescence colorimetric biosensor (AP-Pd1Pt10/CDs) based on red CDs. As shown in Fig. 5c, the emission wavelength intensity of CDs at 608 nm decreases with the increase of dimethoate concentration, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs becomes weaker and weaker with the increase of the concentration of the organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate. The results of Fig. 5d indicated a strong logarithmic correlation between the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 608 nm and the concentration of the organophosphorus pesticide dimethoate, and the standard equation for detection was F = −374.5 logC + 1269.1 (R2 = 0.997), where the corresponding detection range of dimethoate concentration was 0.003–100 mg/L, with a detection limit of 0.41 ng/mL. The inset of Fig. 5c is a photograph of the actual sample of AP-Pd1Pt10/CDs for the detection of different concentrations of dimethoate, and it can be found that the color of the mixed solution gradually becomes lighter with the increase of dimethoate concentration.

Table 2 presents a summary of the detection ranges and limits for various OPs using different sensors. AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs/CDs exhibit a broader detection range of 0.003–100 mg/L compared to other sensors, such as Ir NPs (0.03–1.65 mg/L), Fe SAs/N5-pC-4 (0.001–20 mg/L), CDs (0.5–1 mg/L), and TPE-SiO2-MnO2 (0.001–1 mg/L). Furthermore, the detection limits for AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs and AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs/CDs were 0.37 μg/L and 0.41 μg/L, respectively, which were lower than those of other sensors, such as FeNC (0.98 μg/L), AChE-MnO2-TMB (1 μg/L), and TPE-SiO2-MnO2 (1 μg/L). Therefore, the AP-Pd1Pt10 colorimetric fluorescence biosensor and the red-light CDs-based AP-Pd1Pt10 colorimetric fluorescence biosensor exhibit a broad linear detection range along with a low detection limit, allowing for highly sensitive measurements.

We conducted tests involving non-organophosphorus pesticides and various interfering substances, such as fenvalerate, tebuconazole, thiamethoxam, glucose, fructose, and metal ions including Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+ and Ca2+. As illustrated in Fig. 5e and Fig. S10, when the concentration of dimethoate matched that of the interfering agents, only dimethoate elicited a significant change in the biosensor's signal. This observation confirms the excellent selectivity of the AP-Pd1Pt10 biosensor and AP-Pd1Pt10/CDs biosensor. To assess the biosensor's practical applicability, we evaluated different concentrations of dimethoate in extracts from pears, tomatoes, and cucumbers. The results, presented in Fig. 5f, Fig. S11 and Table S1, indicated that the recovery rates for dimethoate ranged from 95 % to 110 % after introducing various concentrations of the pesticide into the fruit and vegetable extracts. In addition, Fig. S12 shows that the AP-Pd1Pt10 biosensor has good reproducibility, and the absorbance of the AP-Pd1Pt10 biosensor is almost 100 % by comparing the results of AP-Pd1Pt10 in different storage time for the detection of 100 mg/L dimethoate, which indicates that the AP-Pd1Pt10 biosensor has excellent stability and reproducibility. It means that the AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs biosensor can be applied to the detection of real samples of dimethoate.

In conclusion, we employed AP as both a reducing agent and stabilizer to synthesize Pd nanoparticle-centered AP-PdmPtn NFs. The resulting PdPt bimetallic NFs exhibit a particle size of approximately 18 nm. The AP-PdmPtn NFs demonstrate oxidase-like activity, and their catalytic kinetics adhere to the Michaelis-Menten equation. During the investigation of the catalytic mechanism, we identified that the reactive oxygen species generated by AP-PdmPtn NFs include 1O₂ and O₂ −. Additionally, we synthesized red CDs in ethanol using p-phenylenediamine and potassium periodic acid, achieving a particle size of 1–2 nm and an emission wavelength of 608 nm at 555 nm, which can be quenched by blue oxTMB. A colorimetric fluorescent biosensor for detecting organophosphorus pesticides was developed based on the capability of AP-Pd1Pt10 NFs to oxidize TMB to oxTMB, which in turn quenches the fluorescence of the CDs. The sensor has a wide detection range of 0.003–100 mg/L, and the biosensor also has excellent detection limits and specificity, and was applied to the determination of dimethoate concentration in fruits and vegetables. The establishment of the organophosphorus pesticide colorimetric fluorescence biosensor provides a new perspective for the application of nanozymes in biosensing and biometrics, and shows great prospects in the field of colorimetric and fluorescent sensing.

Han Zhao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Kaile Cao: Investigation. Tingting Zhang: Investigation. Lijie Zhou: Visualization. Zengsheng Han: Writing – review & editing.
